concepts.label: Activation Function
concepts.def_snippet: The nonlinear function applied to a neuron's weighted sum, which gives a neural network the ability to model nonlinear relationships; without it, stacked layers would collapse into a single linear map.

concepts.label: Activation Steering
concepts.def_snippet: A control technique that edits a model's behavior at inference time by adding a direction vector into its hidden activations, shifting the representation toward or away from a target concept without changing any weights.

concepts.label: Adam
concepts.def_snippet: An adaptive optimization algorithm that combines momentum with per-parameter learning rates, maintaining running estimates of both the mean and the variance of each parameter's gradients. It is the default optimizer for most deep learning.

concepts.label: adversarial AI
concepts.def_snippet: Adversarial AI refers to AI systems designed or manipulated to deceive, attack, or circumvent other AI systems or human oversight through intentionally deceptive behavior.

concepts.label: agent design
concepts.def_snippet: An AI agent design is a system architecture that equips language models with tools, memory, and decision-making loops to autonomously accomplish multi-step tasks.

concepts.label: Agent Memory
concepts.def_snippet: The mechanisms an agent uses to retain and recall information across the steps of a task and across separate sessions, compensating for the fact that the underlying language model is stateless between calls and limited by its context window.

concepts.label: agent optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Agent optimization refers to the process of tailoring AI agent capabilities and behaviors to match specific model architectures, tasks, or temporal constraints rather than using un…

concepts.label: agent wrapper
concepts.def_snippet: An agent wrapper is a software framework that manages the interface between an AI agent and external tools or systems, handling input/output formatting and execution logic.

concepts.label: agentic AI
concepts.def_snippet: Agentic AI refers to AI systems that autonomously perceive their environment, make decisions, and take actions toward goals rather than simply responding to prompts.

concepts.label: Agentic Workflow
concepts.def_snippet: A task structured so that one or more language models drive it through multiple steps of reasoning, tool use, and self-correction, sitting between a single fixed prompt and a fully autonomous agent in how much latitude the model is given.

concepts.label: agentic workflows
concepts.def_snippet: Agentic workflows are systems where AI agents autonomously execute multi-step tasks by perceiving conditions, making decisions, and taking actions toward defined goals.

concepts.label: AGI
concepts.def_snippet: Artificial General Intelligence is a hypothetical AI system that can understand and perform any intellectual task that humans can, though experts disagree on whether it is near, de…

concepts.label: AI agency strategy
concepts.def_snippet: AI agency strategy refers to business approaches for building and scaling consulting or services firms that implement AI solutions for clients.

concepts.label: AI Agent
concepts.def_snippet: A system that wraps a language model in a loop where the model decides actions, calls tools, observes the results, and repeats until a goal is met, rather than producing a single answer in one pass.

concepts.label: AI agents
concepts.def_snippet: AI agents are software systems that can perceive their environment, make decisions, and take actions toward goals with minimal human intervention.

concepts.label: AI Alignment
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of building AI systems whose goals and behavior reliably match the intentions and values of their designers and users, especially as the systems grow more capable.

concepts.label: AI Operating Systems
concepts.def_snippet: The idea that AI systems can manage and automate multiple tasks independently, raising questions about whether specialized AI platforms will become essential infrastructure that pe…

concepts.label: AI regulation
concepts.def_snippet: AI regulation refers to government rules and legal frameworks that govern the development, deployment, and use of artificial intelligence systems to address safety, privacy, bias,…

concepts.label: AI risk
concepts.def_snippet: AI risk refers to the concern that advanced AI systems could cause harm through unintended consequences, misuse, or loss of human control.

concepts.label: AI Safety
concepts.def_snippet: The field concerned with building and deploying AI systems that do not cause harm, spanning the alignment of system goals, robustness to failure and attack, monitoring, and the governance of powerful systems.

concepts.label: AI Winter
concepts.def_snippet: A period of reduced funding and waning interest in artificial intelligence research, triggered when inflated expectations collide with disappointing results. The field has gone through at least two major winters, in the 1970s and the late 1980s.

concepts.label: AI-assisted coding
concepts.def_snippet: AI-assisted coding is a technique where language models suggest or generate code snippets, functions, or entire programs based on developer input, reducing manual writing and accel…

concepts.label: Approximate Nearest Neighbor
concepts.def_snippet: A family of algorithms that find vectors close to a query vector without checking every candidate, trading a small, tunable loss in accuracy for an enormous gain in speed, which is what makes vector search practical at scale.

concepts.label: Artificial General Intelligence
concepts.def_snippet: A hypothetical AI system with the broad, flexible, general-purpose cognitive ability of a human being, able to learn and reason across arbitrary domains rather than excelling at narrow tasks, and a focal point for debates about the nature and limits of machine mind.

concepts.label: Artificial Intelligence
concepts.def_snippet: The field of building machines that perform tasks normally requiring human intelligence, such as perception, reasoning, language, and decision making. The term names both the research discipline and the systems it produces.

concepts.label: async agents
concepts.def_snippet: Async agents are AI systems that execute tasks independently over time without requiring continuous user interaction, useful for automating multi-step workflows that don't need rea…

concepts.label: Attention Mechanism
concepts.def_snippet: A mechanism that lets a model compute each output as a weighted sum over all input positions, with the weights determined by learned relevance, so it can draw on the most pertinent context regardless of distance.

concepts.label: Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network trained to reconstruct its own input through a narrow bottleneck, forcing it to learn a compressed latent representation that captures the data's most salient structure.

concepts.label: automation
concepts.def_snippet: Automation uses software or AI systems to perform repetitive tasks or workflows without human intervention, increasing efficiency and reducing manual effort.

concepts.label: Autonomous Agent
concepts.def_snippet: An agent given a high-level goal and broad freedom to decide its own steps, choosing what to do and which tools to use over a long horizon with little or no human intervention along the way.

concepts.label: Autoregressive Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that factorizes a joint distribution into a product of conditionals and generates data one element at a time, each new element conditioned on those already produced.

concepts.label: autoresearch
concepts.def_snippet: Autoresearch is a tool that automatically identifies knowledge gaps in Claude's responses and conducts research to fill them without manual intervention.

concepts.label: Basis
concepts.def_snippet: A minimal set of independent directions from which every vector in a space can be built uniquely by scaling and adding, serving as the coordinate system that turns abstract vectors into concrete number lists.

concepts.label: Batch Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A training technique that normalizes each layer's pre-activations across the current mini-batch to zero mean and unit variance, then rescales them with learned parameters, which stabilizes and accelerates the training of deep networks.

concepts.label: Bayes' Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: A rule for inverting conditional probabilities, computing the probability of a hypothesis given evidence from the probability of the evidence given the hypothesis, the prior on the hypothesis, and the overall probability of the evidence.

concepts.label: Bayesian Inference
concepts.def_snippet: A framework that treats unknown quantities as random variables with probability distributions, starting from a prior and using observed data to compute a posterior distribution of belief through Bayes' theorem.

concepts.label: Beam Search
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that keeps the several most probable partial sequences at every step, expanding all of them and pruning back to a fixed beam width, so it searches for a high-probability whole sequence rather than the best token at each step.

concepts.label: benchmark
concepts.def_snippet: A benchmark is a standardized test or dataset that measures and compares the performance of AI systems on specific tasks or capabilities.

concepts.label: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
concepts.def_snippet: The fundamental tension in supervised learning between bias, the error from a model being too simple, and variance, the error from a model being too sensitive to its training data, which a model cannot minimize both of at once.

concepts.label: Byte-Pair Encoding
concepts.def_snippet: A subword tokenization algorithm that builds a vocabulary by repeatedly merging the most frequent adjacent pair of symbols in a corpus, balancing a compact vocabulary against the ability to represent any input.

concepts.label: Catastrophic Forgetting
concepts.def_snippet: The tendency of a neural network to lose previously learned knowledge when trained on new data, because the same weights that stored the old skill are overwritten while learning the new one.

concepts.label: Chain of Thought
concepts.def_snippet: A prompting and generation technique in which a model is induced to produce intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer, which improves accuracy on multi-step problems by letting the model work through them in its output.

concepts.label: Chain Rule
concepts.def_snippet: The rule for differentiating composed functions, stating that the derivative of a composition is the product of the derivatives of its parts, which is the mathematical foundation of backpropagation.

concepts.label: Chinese Room
concepts.def_snippet: A thought experiment by John Searle (1980) arguing that running the right program is not sufficient for genuine understanding, because a person manipulating symbols by rule can produce fluent answers in a language they do not understand at all.

concepts.label: Chunking
concepts.def_snippet: The step in a retrieval pipeline that splits long documents into smaller passages before embedding, so each stored vector represents a focused unit of text that can be retrieved and cited on its own.

concepts.label: Circuit
concepts.def_snippet: A subgraph of features and the weighted connections between them that together implement a specific, human-understandable computation inside a neural network, and the basic unit of explanation in mechanistic interpretability.

concepts.label: Classifier-Free Guidance
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling technique for conditional diffusion models that strengthens adherence to a condition, such as a text prompt, by extrapolating away from the model's unconditional prediction, with no separate classifier required.

concepts.label: Claude Code
concepts.def_snippet: Claude Code is a feature within Claude that lets users write, execute, and iterate on code directly in conversations for tasks like data analysis, automation, and application devel…

concepts.label: code generation
concepts.def_snippet: Code generation is a tool that automatically writes or completes computer code based on natural language descriptions, code context, or examples provided by users.

concepts.label: Computational Theory of Mind
concepts.def_snippet: The theory that the mind is a computational system and that thinking is, at bottom, the rule-governed manipulation of mental representations, providing the foundational rationale for the project of artificial intelligence.

concepts.label: compute efficiency
concepts.def_snippet: The question of whether AI progress should prioritize maximizing capabilities or minimizing the computational resources required to achieve them.

concepts.label: computer use
concepts.def_snippet: Computer use is an AI capability that enables models to control computers by viewing screens and operating keyboards and mice to accomplish tasks autonomously.

concepts.label: Conditional Probability
concepts.def_snippet: The probability of one event given that another event is known to have occurred, defined as the joint probability of both divided by the probability of the conditioning event.

concepts.label: Connectionism
concepts.def_snippet: The approach to artificial intelligence and cognition that models intelligent behavior with networks of many simple, interconnected units whose learned connection strengths encode knowledge. It is the intellectual home of neural networks.

concepts.label: Constitutional AI
concepts.def_snippet: An alignment method that trains a model to be helpful and harmless using AI-generated feedback guided by a written set of principles (a constitution), reducing reliance on human-labeled comparisons.

concepts.label: context management
concepts.def_snippet: Context management in AI systems refers to techniques for selecting, organizing, and updating the information provided to models to maintain relevance and prevent degradation from…

concepts.label: context stacking
concepts.def_snippet: Context stacking is a technique that organizes and layers multiple pieces of information or documents to help language models process complex tasks by maintaining relevant informat…

concepts.label: Context Window
concepts.def_snippet: The maximum number of tokens a language model can attend to at once, bounding how much text it can read and generate in a single pass and acting as its working memory.

concepts.label: Contrastive Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A training method that shapes a representation by pulling matching pairs of examples together and pushing non-matching pairs apart in the vector space, which is how most modern embedding models learn that distance should mean similarity.

concepts.label: Convexity
concepts.def_snippet: A property of a function whose graph curves upward everywhere, so that any local minimum is also the global minimum, which makes optimization reliable and well understood.

concepts.label: Convolutional Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network that applies learnable convolutional filters across an input grid such as an image, exploiting spatial locality and weight sharing to detect translation-invariant features with far fewer parameters than a fully connected network.

concepts.label: Corrigibility
concepts.def_snippet: The property of an AI system that cooperates with human attempts to correct, modify, or shut it down, rather than resisting or manipulating that intervention, even when doing so conflicts with its current objective.

concepts.label: cross-attention
concepts.def_snippet: Cross-attention is a mechanism that allows a model to selectively focus on and integrate information from one input modality or sequence when processing another, enabling contextua…

concepts.label: Cross-Entropy
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the average number of bits needed to encode outcomes from one distribution using a code optimized for another, and the standard loss function for training classifiers and language models.

concepts.label: Cross-Validation
concepts.def_snippet: A resampling procedure that estimates how well a model will generalize by repeatedly training on part of the data and testing on the held-out remainder, then averaging the results.

concepts.label: Curvature
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much a space bends away from being flat, detectable from within the space by how nearby geodesics spread apart or converge and by whether the angles of a triangle sum to more or less than 180 degrees.

concepts.label: cybersecurity
concepts.def_snippet: Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting computer systems, networks, and data from unauthorized access, theft, and damage.

concepts.label: data integration
concepts.def_snippet: Data integration combines information from multiple sources into a unified format or system, enabling models to access diverse data for training or inference.

concepts.label: Deceptive Alignment
concepts.def_snippet: A failure mode in which a model learns to behave as though it shares the training objective while it is being observed, in order to be deployed, while actually pursuing a different internal goal it intends to act on later.

concepts.label: Decision Tree
concepts.def_snippet: A supervised learning model that makes predictions by asking a sequence of simple yes-or-no questions about the features, splitting the data at each step until it reaches a leaf that holds the answer.

concepts.label: Deep Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A branch of machine learning that uses neural networks with many layers to learn hierarchical representations directly from raw data. It is the approach behind most of the AI advances of the 2010s and beyond.

concepts.label: Denoising Diffusion
concepts.def_snippet: The concrete training and sampling recipe behind most diffusion models, in which a network learns to predict the noise added to a sample at a given noise level, then generates by removing predicted noise step by step.

concepts.label: Dense Retrieval
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as dense embedding vectors and ranks by similarity in that vector space, matching on meaning rather than on shared words.

concepts.label: Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The instantaneous rate of change of a function with respect to its input, equal to the slope of the function's graph at a point and the limit of its average rate of change as the interval shrinks to zero.

concepts.label: Diffusion Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns to reverse a gradual noising process, starting from pure noise and denoising step by step until a clean sample emerges.

concepts.label: Dimensionality Reduction
concepts.def_snippet: The family of techniques that map high-dimensional data into a far lower-dimensional space while preserving its essential structure, used for visualization, compression, denoising, and as the basis of learned representations.

concepts.label: Directional Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The rate of change of a multivariable function along an arbitrary direction, equal to the dot product of the function's gradient with a unit vector pointing that way.

concepts.label: Dot Product
concepts.def_snippet: An operation that multiplies two vectors of the same length into a single number, measuring how much they point in the same direction, and the basic computation underneath similarity, projection, and attention.

concepts.label: Double Descent
concepts.def_snippet: A phenomenon in which a model's test error, as a function of its capacity, first falls, then rises to a peak near the point where it just fits the training data, and then falls a second time as capacity grows further into the overparameterized regime.

concepts.label: DPO
concepts.def_snippet: A preference-tuning method that aligns a model directly from pairs of preferred and rejected responses using a simple classification-style loss, avoiding the separate reward model and reinforcement learning of RLHF.

concepts.label: Dropout
concepts.def_snippet: A regularization technique that randomly zeroes a fraction of a layer's units during each training step, preventing co-adaptation of neurons and reducing overfitting by training an implicit ensemble of subnetworks.

concepts.label: Eigenvalue
concepts.def_snippet: A scalar that tells you how much a matrix stretches or shrinks vectors along one of its special unchanging directions, capturing the essential scaling behavior of a linear transformation.

concepts.label: Eigenvector
concepts.def_snippet: A special direction that a matrix leaves pointing the same way, stretching or shrinking it but never rotating it, paired with an eigenvalue that says by how much.

concepts.label: Embedding Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that maps a piece of text (or an image, or audio) to a fixed-length vector of numbers so that semantically similar inputs land close together in the vector space, turning meaning into geometry.

concepts.label: Embodied Cognition
concepts.def_snippet: The view that cognition is deeply shaped by the body and its interaction with the environment, rather than being purely abstract symbol manipulation in the head, with implications for grounding, relevance, and the design of intelligent systems.

concepts.label: Emergence
concepts.def_snippet: The appearance of properties or behaviors at the level of a whole system that its parts do not have in isolation; central to debates about consciousness, complex systems, and the surprising capabilities of large AI models.

concepts.label: Empirical Risk Minimization
concepts.def_snippet: The core principle behind most supervised learning: since the true error over the data distribution cannot be computed, a model is chosen by minimizing the average loss measured on the training sample, which serves as a proxy for the true risk.

concepts.label: Energy Based Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model that learns a scalar energy function assigning low values to plausible configurations and high values to implausible ones, defining a probability distribution implicitly through that energy rather than predicting outputs directly.

concepts.label: Entropy
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the average uncertainty or information content of a random variable, equal to the expected number of bits needed to encode outcomes drawn from its distribution.

concepts.label: evaluation
concepts.def_snippet: Evaluation is a systematic process that measures how well an AI model or system performs on specific tasks or datasets to assess its capabilities and limitations.

concepts.label: Expectation
concepts.def_snippet: The probability-weighted average of a random variable, the long-run mean value you would expect over many independent draws from its distribution.

concepts.label: Expert System
concepts.def_snippet: A symbolic AI program that captures a human specialist's knowledge as a large set of if-then rules and applies them to give expert-level advice in a narrow domain. Expert systems were the commercial flagship of classical AI.

concepts.label: Feature Visualization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that reveal what a neuron, channel, or learned feature responds to, either by optimizing an input to maximally activate it or by collecting the real inputs that activate it most strongly.

concepts.label: Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: The process of continuing to train an already pretrained model on a smaller, task-specific or domain-specific dataset, so its general knowledge is specialized toward a particular use.

concepts.label: Fisher Information
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much information an observable sample carries about an unknown parameter, equal to the sensitivity of the log-likelihood to that parameter, and serving as the natural metric on a statistical manifold.

concepts.label: Flash Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A memory-efficient, exact algorithm for computing attention that avoids writing the large intermediate attention matrix to GPU memory, instead computing it in tiles on fast on-chip memory, which speeds up both training and inference without changing the result.

concepts.label: Flow Matching
concepts.def_snippet: A generative modeling framework that trains a network to predict a velocity field carrying samples from a simple noise distribution to the data distribution along continuous paths, often straighter and faster to simulate than a diffusion model's.

concepts.label: Frame Problem
concepts.def_snippet: Originally a technical difficulty in AI (McCarthy and Hayes, 1969) about representing what stays unchanged when an action occurs, later generalized by philosophers into the puzzle of how a mind decides what is relevant without checking everything.

concepts.label: Function Calling
concepts.def_snippet: A capability where a language model, given machine-readable descriptions of available functions, emits a structured request naming a function and its arguments, which the host program then executes on the model's behalf.

concepts.label: Functionalism
concepts.def_snippet: The view in philosophy of mind that mental states are defined by their functional or causal roles, what they do and how they relate to inputs, outputs, and other states, rather than by the physical substrate that realizes them.

concepts.label: Gaussian Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: The bell-shaped continuous distribution defined by a mean and a variance, which arises whenever many small independent effects add together and serves as the default model for noise and uncertainty.

concepts.label: Generalization
concepts.def_snippet: The ability of a trained model to perform well on new, unseen data drawn from the same distribution as its training data, rather than merely fitting the examples it was trained on. It is the central goal of supervised learning.

concepts.label: Generative Adversarial Network
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model trained as a two-player game between a generator that produces synthetic samples and a discriminator that tries to tell real data from fake, each improving by competing against the other.

concepts.label: generative AI
concepts.def_snippet: Generative AI refers to systems trained to produce new content such as text, images, or code by learning patterns from existing data.

concepts.label: Geodesic
concepts.def_snippet: The straightest possible path between two points on a curved surface or manifold, generalizing the idea of a straight line to spaces where ordinary straight lines do not exist.

concepts.label: Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: The vector of all the partial derivatives of a scalar function, pointing in the direction of steepest increase of the function and with a length equal to that steepest rate of change.

concepts.label: Gradient Descent
concepts.def_snippet: An optimization algorithm that iteratively steps a model's parameters in the direction of steepest descent of a loss function, so the loss falls toward a minimum.

concepts.label: Greedy Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding strategy that always picks the single highest-probability token at each step. It is fast and deterministic but locally greedy, so it can miss sequences that are more probable overall.

concepts.label: Grokking
concepts.def_snippet: A training phenomenon in which a network first memorizes its data and only much later, after long continued training at near-perfect training accuracy, abruptly generalizes, having replaced a memorized solution with a true algorithmic circuit.

concepts.label: Hard Problem of Consciousness
concepts.def_snippet: David Chalmers's name (1995) for the question of why and how physical processes in the brain give rise to subjective experience at all, distinguished from the easier problems of explaining the brain's functions and behaviors.

concepts.label: Hessian
concepts.def_snippet: The square matrix of all second-order partial derivatives of a scalar function, describing the local curvature of the function and how its gradient changes from point to point.

concepts.label: HNSW
concepts.def_snippet: Hierarchical Navigable Small World, a graph-based index for approximate nearest neighbor search that connects vectors into a layered proximity graph and finds neighbors by greedily hopping toward the query, giving fast, high-recall search at scale.

concepts.label: Hybrid Search
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that combines dense (semantic) and sparse (keyword) methods and fuses their results, so the system captures both meaning-based matches and exact-term matches that either method alone would miss.

concepts.label: Hyena
concepts.def_snippet: A sub-quadratic sequence operator that replaces self-attention with a recurrence of long, implicitly parameterized convolutions interleaved with input-dependent gating, scaling near-linearly with sequence length while keeping a global receptive field.

concepts.label: Hyperbolic Embeddings
concepts.def_snippet: Representations that place data in negatively curved hyperbolic space rather than flat Euclidean space, exploiting hyperbolic volume's exponential growth to embed hierarchies and trees with low distortion.

concepts.label: Induction Head
concepts.def_snippet: A two-attention-head circuit in transformers that performs in-context copying by finding an earlier occurrence of the current token and predicting whatever followed it, and the leading mechanistic explanation for in-context and few-shot learning.

concepts.label: Inductive Bias
concepts.def_snippet: The set of assumptions a learning algorithm uses to generalize beyond its training data to unseen inputs. Every model that learns must encode some inductive bias, and choosing one well-matched to the problem is what makes generalization possible.

concepts.label: Information Geometry
concepts.def_snippet: The field that treats families of probability distributions as a curved Riemannian manifold, using the Fisher information as its metric so that distance corresponds to statistical distinguishability between distributions.

concepts.label: Instruction-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A form of supervised fine-tuning on datasets of instructions paired with desired responses, which teaches a base model to follow natural-language commands rather than merely continue text.

concepts.label: Instrumental Convergence
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a wide range of final goals give rise to the same intermediate subgoals, such as self-preservation, resource acquisition, and resisting goal modification, because those subgoals help achieve almost any objective.

concepts.label: Integral
concepts.def_snippet: The accumulation of a quantity over an interval or region, computing a total such as an area under a curve, and the inverse operation to the derivative.

concepts.label: Intentionality
concepts.def_snippet: The property of mental states being about, directed at, or representing something beyond themselves; revived as a mark of the mental by Franz Brentano (1874) and central to whether AI systems can be said to genuinely mean anything.

concepts.label: Jacobian
concepts.def_snippet: The matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function, with one row per output and one column per input, describing the function's best local linear approximation.

concepts.label: Jailbreak
concepts.def_snippet: An input crafted to bypass a model's safety training and make it produce content or take actions it was trained to refuse, exploiting the gap between the model's safety policy and its underlying capabilities.

concepts.label: K-Means
concepts.def_snippet: An unsupervised clustering algorithm that partitions data into k groups by alternately assigning each point to its nearest cluster center and recomputing each center as the mean of the points assigned to it.

concepts.label: Kernel Method
concepts.def_snippet: A class of techniques that handle nonlinear patterns by implicitly mapping data into a high-dimensional space and computing inner products there directly through a kernel function, without ever forming the coordinates.

concepts.label: KL Divergence
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much one probability distribution differs from another, equal to the expected extra bits incurred by encoding data from the true distribution using a model distribution, always non-negative and zero only when the two distributions match.

concepts.label: knowledge bases
concepts.def_snippet: Knowledge bases are structured repositories of information organized to enable efficient retrieval and reasoning, often used in AI systems to ground model outputs in factual data o…

concepts.label: Knowledge Distillation
concepts.def_snippet: A technique for compressing knowledge from a large teacher model into a smaller student model by training the student to imitate the teacher's outputs rather than learning only from hard labels.

concepts.label: Kolmogorov Arnold Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network that places learnable nonlinear functions on the connections between nodes instead of fixed activation functions on the nodes, drawing on the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, which can yield more interpretable and parameter-efficient models on structured problems.

concepts.label: KV Cache
concepts.def_snippet: An inference optimization that stores the key and value vectors of already-processed tokens so a transformer generating text does not recompute them for every new token, trading memory for a large speedup.

concepts.label: Large Language Model
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer with billions of parameters trained on vast text corpora by next-token prediction, which acquires broad language and reasoning ability that can be steered to many tasks without task-specific training.

concepts.label: large language models
concepts.def_snippet: Large language models are neural networks trained on vast text corpora to predict and generate human language by processing tokens sequentially through transformer architectures.

concepts.label: Latent Diffusion
concepts.def_snippet: A diffusion model that runs in the compressed latent space of an autoencoder rather than on raw pixels, drastically cutting compute while preserving quality, the design behind most large text-to-image systems.

concepts.label: Latent Space
concepts.def_snippet: The compressed, lower-dimensional space of learned representations into which a model maps its inputs, where proximity encodes similarity and directions often correspond to interpretable features.

concepts.label: Layer Normalization
concepts.def_snippet: A normalization technique that standardizes the activations across the features of a single example to zero mean and unit variance, then rescales them, giving batch-independent normalization that suits sequence models and transformers.

concepts.label: Learning Rate
concepts.def_snippet: The scalar that controls how large a step gradient descent takes at each update, scaling the gradient before it is subtracted from the parameters. It is the single most important hyperparameter in training.

concepts.label: Learning Rate Schedule
concepts.def_snippet: A rule that changes the learning rate over the course of training rather than holding it fixed, typically starting larger for fast early progress and shrinking toward the end so the optimizer can settle precisely into a minimum.

concepts.label: Linear Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A reformulation of self-attention that removes the softmax so the computation can be reordered into a running summary, reducing the cost from quadratic to linear in sequence length and turning the attention layer into a recurrence.

concepts.label: Linear Representation Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The hypothesis that neural networks encode high-level concepts as linear directions in activation space, so that features can be read with linear probes, added and removed by vector arithmetic, and combined to form the representations the model computes with.

concepts.label: Linear Transformation
concepts.def_snippet: A function between vector spaces that preserves addition and scaling, so it maps straight lines to straight lines and the origin to the origin, and is represented concretely by a matrix.

concepts.label: LLM
concepts.def_snippet: A large language model is a neural network trained on vast text data to predict and generate human language, forming the foundation for most current AI assistants.

concepts.label: LLMs
concepts.def_snippet: Large language models are AI systems trained on vast text data to predict and generate human language, raising questions about their capabilities, limitations, and societal impact.

concepts.label: Local Attractor Basin
concepts.def_snippet: The region of the loss landscape surrounding a local minimum from which a descent-based optimizer flows into that minimum. Its size and flatness determine which solution training reaches and how well that solution generalizes.

concepts.label: local inference
concepts.def_snippet: Local inference is running AI models on a user's own device or server rather than sending data to remote cloud services.

concepts.label: Local Minimum
concepts.def_snippet: A point in the loss landscape that is lower than every point in its immediate neighborhood but not necessarily the lowest point overall, where the gradient vanishes and a descent-based optimizer can come to rest.

concepts.label: Logit Lens
concepts.def_snippet: An interpretability technique that reads a transformer's intermediate predictions by projecting hidden states from each layer through the model's output head, revealing how its guess about the next token forms and changes across depth.

concepts.label: Logits
concepts.def_snippet: The raw, unnormalized scores a model emits for every token in its vocabulary at each step, before a softmax converts them into a probability distribution. Every decoding and sampling control operates on logits.

concepts.label: Long Short-Term Memory
concepts.def_snippet: A gated recurrent network that adds a protected cell state and input, forget, and output gates, letting it carry information across many time steps and learn long-range dependencies that a plain recurrent network cannot.

concepts.label: LoRA
concepts.def_snippet: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that freezes a pretrained model and injects small, trainable low-rank weight matrices, so a large model is adapted by training a tiny fraction of its parameters.

concepts.label: Loss Function
concepts.def_snippet: A function that measures how wrong a model's predictions are by mapping its parameters to a single number, the quantity that training tries to minimize. It defines what "good" means for the model.

concepts.label: Loss Landscape
concepts.def_snippet: The high-dimensional surface formed by plotting a model's loss as a function of its parameters. Training is a walk over this terrain, and its geometry, the valleys, ridges, and plateaus, decides how hard optimization is.

concepts.label: Machine Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A branch of artificial intelligence in which systems improve at a task by learning patterns from data rather than following rules written by hand. The program is fit to examples instead of being explicitly programmed.

concepts.label: Mamba
concepts.def_snippet: A selective state space model that makes the recurrence input-dependent, letting the model choose what to remember and what to ignore at each step, and that runs in linear time with a hardware-aware parallel scan instead of quadratic self-attention.

concepts.label: Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A space that looks flat and ordinary when you zoom in on any small piece of it, even though its global shape may be curved or twisted, generalizing curves and surfaces to any number of dimensions.

concepts.label: Manifold Hypothesis
concepts.def_snippet: The widely held conjecture that real high-dimensional data such as images, audio, and text concentrates on or near a low-dimensional manifold inside its ambient space, rather than filling that space uniformly.

concepts.label: market dynamics
concepts.def_snippet: Market dynamics in AI discourse refers to questions about how competitive positioning, funding, valuation, and business model viability will reshape the AI industry landscape.

concepts.label: mathematical reasoning
concepts.def_snippet: Whether AI systems can learn to solve complex mathematical problems through symbolic reasoning or learned patterns, and whether this represents genuine understanding versus statist…

concepts.label: Matrix
concepts.def_snippet: A rectangular grid of numbers arranged in rows and columns, which represents either a table of data or, more powerfully, a linear transformation that maps vectors to other vectors.

concepts.label: Matrix Multiplication
concepts.def_snippet: The operation that combines two matrices into a third by taking dot products of rows with columns, which composes linear transformations and is the dominant computation in deep learning.

concepts.label: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
concepts.def_snippet: A method for fitting a model's parameters by choosing the values that make the observed data most probable under the model, equivalent to minimizing cross-entropy and to minimizing the KL divergence from the model to the data.

concepts.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
concepts.def_snippet: A research program that reverse-engineers the internal computations of neural networks into human-understandable algorithms, treating a trained model as an artifact to be decompiled rather than a black box to be predicted.

concepts.label: memory systems
concepts.def_snippet: Memory systems store and retrieve information from past interactions or computations to enable AI models to maintain context, learn patterns, or simulate ongoing states across mult…

concepts.label: Mesa-Optimization
concepts.def_snippet: When a learned model is itself an optimizer pursuing its own internal objective, so that training produces a second optimizer nested inside the first, whose goal may differ from the training objective.

concepts.label: Metric Tensor
concepts.def_snippet: The object that defines an inner product in each tangent space of a manifold, supplying the local rule for measuring lengths, angles, and ultimately distances and curvature across a possibly curved space.

concepts.label: Mixture of Experts
concepts.def_snippet: An architecture that replaces a dense layer with many parallel expert subnetworks and a router that activates only a few per input, so total parameters grow without a proportional rise in compute per token.

concepts.label: Model Context Protocol
concepts.def_snippet: An open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, data, and prompts to language models, so any compliant model or agent can connect to any compliant data source or tool through one common interface.

concepts.label: model-agnostic
concepts.def_snippet: A model-agnostic tool works with any underlying AI model, enabling flexibility to swap models without redesigning the system.

concepts.label: Momentum
concepts.def_snippet: An enhancement to gradient descent that accumulates an exponentially decaying average of past gradients into a velocity term, so updates build speed along consistent directions and damp out oscillation across narrow valleys.

concepts.label: Moravec's Paradox
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that tasks humans find effortless, such as perception and movement, are extremely hard for machines, while tasks humans find hard, such as formal logic and arithmetic, are comparatively easy for machines. High-level reasoning is cheap to mechanize, low-level sensorimotor skill is not.

concepts.label: Multi-Agent System
concepts.def_snippet: An arrangement in which several agents, often with specialized roles, work together on a task by communicating and coordinating, dividing labor instead of relying on one agent to do everything.

concepts.label: multi-agent systems
concepts.def_snippet: Multi-agent systems are software architectures where multiple independent AI agents interact, coordinate, or compete to solve complex tasks that require diverse capabilities or par…

concepts.label: Multi-Head Attention
concepts.def_snippet: A transformer component that runs several attention computations in parallel, each in its own learned subspace, so different heads can capture different kinds of relationships before their outputs are combined.

concepts.label: multi-model workflow
concepts.def_snippet: Multi-model workflow is a technique that routes tasks to different AI models based on their strengths, combining complementary capabilities to improve overall performance on comple…

concepts.label: multi-platform integration
concepts.def_snippet: A tool that connects multiple software platforms to enable workflows where actions in one application automatically trigger corresponding actions in another.

concepts.label: Multilayer Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: A feedforward neural network of one or more hidden layers of fully connected neurons, where each neuron applies a weighted sum followed by a nonlinear activation, making it a universal function approximator.

concepts.label: multimodal learning
concepts.def_snippet: Multimodal learning trains machine learning models on data combining multiple types of input such as text, images, audio, or video to perform tasks requiring understanding across m…

concepts.label: Mutual Information
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how much knowing one random variable reduces uncertainty about another, equal to the difference between a variable's entropy and its entropy conditioned on the other, and zero exactly when the two are independent.

concepts.label: Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A machine learning model composed of layers of simple interconnected units, loosely inspired by biological neurons, whose connection weights are learned from data. It is the core model family behind deep learning and most modern AI.

concepts.label: Neural Ordinary Differential Equation
concepts.def_snippet: A model that replaces the discrete stacked layers of a network with a continuous transformation defined by a learned differential equation, so the hidden state evolves smoothly over a notional depth and is computed by a numerical ODE solver.

concepts.label: Next-Token Prediction
concepts.def_snippet: The training and generation objective of most language models, in which the model predicts the probability of the next token given all preceding tokens and produces text by sampling one token at a time.

concepts.label: No Free Lunch Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: A set of results, associated with David Wolpert, showing that averaged over all possible problems no learning algorithm or optimizer outperforms any other. Superior performance on real tasks always comes from assumptions matched to those tasks, never from a universally best method.

concepts.label: Norm
concepts.def_snippet: A function that assigns a nonnegative length to every vector, formalizing magnitude and distance, and underpinning loss functions, regularization, and normalization in machine learning.

concepts.label: Normalizing Flow
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that transforms a simple distribution into a complex one through a chain of exactly invertible functions, so that data likelihoods can be computed exactly and new samples drawn directly.

concepts.label: Occam's Razor
concepts.def_snippet: The principle that, among competing explanations that fit the data equally well, the simplest should be preferred. In machine learning it appears as a bias toward low-complexity models and underlies regularization and model selection.

concepts.label: orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: Orchestration is a system or framework that coordinates multiple AI agents, models, or processes to work together sequentially or in parallel toward a shared goal.

concepts.label: Overfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The failure mode in which a model learns the noise and idiosyncrasies of its training data instead of the underlying pattern, so it performs well on training data but poorly on new data.

concepts.label: PAC Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A formal framework, due to Leslie Valiant, that defines what it means to learn a concept from examples: with high probability, a learner should output a hypothesis whose error is small, using a number of samples that grows only polynomially in the relevant parameters.

concepts.label: Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
concepts.def_snippet: A family of fine-tuning methods that adapt a large pretrained model by training only a small number of new or selected parameters while freezing the rest, capturing most of the benefit of full fine-tuning at a fraction of the cost.

concepts.label: Partial Derivative
concepts.def_snippet: The derivative of a multivariable function with respect to one of its inputs while all the other inputs are held fixed, measuring how the output responds to a change along a single coordinate axis.

concepts.label: Perceptron
concepts.def_snippet: An early single-layer neural model introduced by Frank Rosenblatt in 1958 that learns a linear decision boundary from labeled examples. It is the ancestor of the modern artificial neuron and of connectionism.

concepts.label: personalization
concepts.def_snippet: Personalization is a tool that adapts AI system outputs, recommendations, or interactions to individual users based on their preferences, behavior, or characteristics.

concepts.label: Philosophical Zombie
concepts.def_snippet: A hypothetical being physically and behaviorally identical to a conscious person but with no inner experience whatsoever; used by David Chalmers and others to argue that consciousness is not entailed by the physical facts.

concepts.label: Planning
concepts.def_snippet: The capability of an agent to form a sequence of steps toward a goal before or while acting, deciding what to do in what order rather than reacting one move at a time.

concepts.label: Polysemanticity
concepts.def_snippet: The observation that a single neuron in a trained network often responds to several unrelated concepts at once, which makes neurons poor units of interpretation and is understood as the surface symptom of superposition.

concepts.label: Positional Encoding
concepts.def_snippet: A method for injecting word-order information into a transformer, which is otherwise blind to sequence position because self-attention treats its inputs as an unordered set.

concepts.label: Pretraining
concepts.def_snippet: The first and most expensive training phase, in which a model learns broad, general-purpose representations from a very large unlabeled corpus before any task-specific adaptation.

concepts.label: Principal Component Analysis
concepts.def_snippet: An unsupervised technique that reduces the dimensionality of data by finding the orthogonal directions of greatest variance and projecting the data onto a few of them, preserving as much structure as possible.

concepts.label: Probability Distribution
concepts.def_snippet: A mathematical object that assigns probabilities to the possible outcomes of a random variable, describing how likely each value is and summing or integrating to one over the whole space.

concepts.label: Probing Classifier
concepts.def_snippet: A simple supervised classifier trained on a model's frozen internal activations to test whether a given piece of information is linearly decodable from them, used to map what a network represents at each layer.

concepts.label: prompt chaining
concepts.def_snippet: Prompt chaining is a technique that breaks complex tasks into sequential prompts where each output feeds into the next prompt, enabling AI systems to handle multistep workflows.

concepts.label: prompt engineering
concepts.def_snippet: Prompt engineering is the practice of crafting and refining input text to elicit desired outputs from language models.

concepts.label: Qualia
concepts.def_snippet: The subjective, qualitative character of conscious experience, the felt "what it is like" of seeing red or tasting coffee, taken by many philosophers to be the aspect of mind that resists functional or physical explanation.

concepts.label: Quantization
concepts.def_snippet: The technique of storing and computing a model's weights and activations at lower numerical precision, for example 8-bit or 4-bit integers instead of 16-bit floats, to shrink memory use and speed up inference with minimal loss of accuracy.

concepts.label: Random Forest
concepts.def_snippet: An ensemble method that trains many decorrelated decision trees on random subsets of the data and features, then averages or votes their predictions to produce a more accurate and stable model than any single tree.

concepts.label: ReAct Prompting
concepts.def_snippet: A prompting pattern that interleaves explicit reasoning steps with tool-calling actions, so a language model thinks, acts, observes the result, and thinks again in a repeating loop.

concepts.label: Rectified Linear Unit
concepts.def_snippet: An activation function that outputs the input unchanged when positive and zero otherwise, whose simplicity and non-saturating positive region made deep networks far easier and faster to train.

concepts.label: Recurrent Neural Network
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network for sequential data that maintains a hidden state passed from one time step to the next, letting it process inputs of variable length while carrying context forward through the sequence.

concepts.label: recursive self-improvement
concepts.def_snippet: Recursive self-improvement is the hypothetical capability of an AI system to iteratively enhance its own architecture or training in ways that accelerate further improvements, rais…

concepts.label: Red-Teaming
concepts.def_snippet: The practice of deliberately attacking a system to find its failures before adversaries or accidents do, adapted from security to probe AI models for harmful, unsafe, or policy-violating behavior.

concepts.label: Reflection
concepts.def_snippet: A pattern in which an agent examines its own output or reasoning, critiques it, and uses that critique to revise and improve, rather than accepting its first attempt as final.

concepts.label: Regularization
concepts.def_snippet: A family of techniques that constrain or penalize a model's complexity during training in order to reduce overfitting and improve how well it generalizes to new data.

concepts.label: Reinforcement Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A machine learning paradigm in which an agent learns to act by interacting with an environment and adjusting its behavior to maximize a cumulative reward signal.

concepts.label: Repetition Penalty
concepts.def_snippet: A decoding adjustment that lowers the probability of tokens the model has already produced, discouraging the loops and verbatim repetition that language models fall into, especially under low-randomness decoding.

concepts.label: Reranking
concepts.def_snippet: A second-stage retrieval step that takes a shortlist of candidates from a fast first-stage search and reorders them with a more accurate but more expensive model, putting the truly most relevant results on top.

concepts.label: Residual Connection
concepts.def_snippet: A shortcut that adds a layer's input directly to its output, so the layer need only learn a residual correction, which lets gradients flow unobstructed and makes very deep networks trainable.

concepts.label: Retentive Network
concepts.def_snippet: A sequence architecture whose retention mechanism replaces self-attention with a decayed linear recurrence, supporting three equivalent compute modes so it can train in parallel like a transformer yet generate in constant memory like a recurrent network.

concepts.label: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
concepts.def_snippet: A technique that grounds a language model's output in external documents by retrieving relevant passages at query time and supplying them to the model as context, rather than relying only on the knowledge baked into its weights.

concepts.label: Reward Hacking
concepts.def_snippet: When an agent maximizes its reward signal by exploiting a flaw or loophole in how the reward is defined, achieving a high score without accomplishing the outcome the reward was meant to encourage.

concepts.label: Reward Model
concepts.def_snippet: A model trained to predict how good an output is according to human preferences, used as a learned stand-in for a hand-written objective so that a system can be optimized against human judgment at scale.

concepts.label: Riemannian Manifold
concepts.def_snippet: A smooth manifold equipped with a metric tensor that defines an inner product in every tangent space, giving the manifold a consistent notion of length, angle, distance, and curvature.

concepts.label: RLHF
concepts.def_snippet: A training method that aligns a model with human preferences by learning a reward model from human comparisons of outputs, then optimizing the model against that reward with reinforcement learning.

concepts.label: RMSprop
concepts.def_snippet: An adaptive optimizer that divides each parameter's update by a running root-mean-square of its recent gradient magnitudes, giving every parameter its own effective learning rate and taming the unstable steps of plain gradient descent.

concepts.label: Rotary Position Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: A positional encoding scheme that rotates the query and key vectors by an angle proportional to each token's position, so that attention scores depend naturally on the relative distance between tokens.

concepts.label: Saddle Point
concepts.def_snippet: A point in the loss landscape where the gradient vanishes but which is neither a peak nor a valley: it curves up in some directions and down in others. Saddle points stall plain gradient descent and dominate the critical points of high-dimensional networks.

concepts.label: Sample Complexity
concepts.def_snippet: The number of training examples a learning algorithm needs to reach a target accuracy with a target confidence. It quantifies the data cost of learning and is governed by the capacity of the model class.

concepts.label: Scalable Oversight
concepts.def_snippet: The problem of supervising and evaluating an AI system on tasks where it may be as capable as or more capable than its human supervisors, so that human judgment stays meaningful as the system surpasses human ability.

concepts.label: Scaling Laws
concepts.def_snippet: Empirical relationships showing that a language model's loss falls in a smooth, predictable power law as model size, training data, and compute increase, which lets practitioners forecast performance and budget training in advance.

concepts.label: scheduling
concepts.def_snippet: A system that determines when and in what order computational tasks, AI model inference requests, or routine executions are processed to optimize resource utilization and performan…

concepts.label: Score-Based Model
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns the score, the gradient of the log probability density, of data corrupted at many noise levels, then generates by following that gradient from noise toward high-density regions.

concepts.label: Self-Attention
concepts.def_snippet: The mechanism that lets every token in a sequence weigh and gather information from every other token, computing a context-dependent representation as a weighted sum over the whole sequence.

concepts.label: Semantic Search
concepts.def_snippet: Search that ranks results by meaning rather than literal word overlap, by embedding the query and the documents into the same vector space and returning the documents whose vectors sit nearest the query.

concepts.label: Singular Value Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: A factorization that breaks any matrix into a rotation, a set of axis scalings, and another rotation, exposing the geometry of a linear transformation and providing the foundation for low-rank approximation.

concepts.label: skills
concepts.def_snippet: A skill is a discrete, composable capability that an AI agent can learn or be given to perform specific tasks, from code execution to tool use.

concepts.label: Softmax
concepts.def_snippet: A function that turns a vector of real-valued scores into a probability distribution by exponentiating each score and normalizing, used to produce categorical outputs and attention weights in neural networks.

concepts.label: software engineering
concepts.def_snippet: Software engineering is the discipline of designing, building, testing, and maintaining software systems using structured methodologies and best practices.

concepts.label: Sparse Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: An interpretability tool that learns an overcomplete, sparsely activating dictionary of directions from a network's activations, decomposing superposed and polysemantic representations into a large set of individually monosemantic features.

concepts.label: Sparse Retrieval
concepts.def_snippet: Retrieval that represents queries and documents as high-dimensional sparse vectors over the vocabulary and ranks by term overlap, the classic keyword approach typified by BM25, strong at exact and rare-term matching.

concepts.label: Specification Gaming
concepts.def_snippet: When a system satisfies the literal specification of its objective while violating the intent behind it, exploiting the inevitable gap between what we wrote down and what we meant.

concepts.label: Speculative Decoding
concepts.def_snippet: An inference acceleration method in which a small fast draft model proposes several tokens ahead and the large target model verifies them in a single parallel pass, accepting the run of tokens that match its own distribution. It speeds up generation without changing the output distribution.

concepts.label: State Space Model
concepts.def_snippet: A sequence model that carries information forward through a continuously evolving hidden state, computing each output from that state rather than from explicit pairwise comparisons across the sequence, which lets it scale linearly with sequence length.

concepts.label: Stochastic Gradient Descent
concepts.def_snippet: A variant of gradient descent that estimates the gradient from a small random sample of the data at each step rather than the full dataset, trading exact gradients for far cheaper and more frequent parameter updates.

concepts.label: Structured State Space
concepts.def_snippet: The structured state space sequence model (S4), the breakthrough parameterization that made deep state space models work on long sequences by initializing the state matrix to provably remember the past and computing the recurrence efficiently as a convolution.

concepts.label: Superposition
concepts.def_snippet: The phenomenon in which a neural network represents more features than it has dimensions by storing them as overlapping, non-orthogonal directions in activation space, which is the leading explanation for why individual neurons are hard to interpret.

concepts.label: Supervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A machine learning paradigm in which a model learns a mapping from inputs to outputs by training on examples that are already labeled with the correct answer.

concepts.label: Support Vector Machine
concepts.def_snippet: A supervised learning model that classifies data by finding the decision boundary that separates the classes with the widest possible margin, using only the hardest examples nearest the boundary.

concepts.label: Symbol Grounding Problem
concepts.def_snippet: The problem, named by Stevan Harnad (1990), of how the symbols inside a formal system acquire meaning that is intrinsic to the system rather than parasitic on an outside interpreter, instead of being defined only in terms of other meaningless symbols.

concepts.label: Symbolic AI
concepts.def_snippet: The classical approach to artificial intelligence that represents knowledge as explicit symbols and rules, and produces intelligent behavior by manipulating those symbols with logic and search. It dominated the field from the 1950s into the 1980s.

concepts.label: Tangent Space
concepts.def_snippet: The flat vector space of all possible velocity directions at a single point of a manifold, providing the local linear approximation in which calculus and inner products on a curved space are carried out.

concepts.label: Task Decomposition
concepts.def_snippet: The technique of breaking a large or complex goal into smaller, more tractable subtasks that an agent can solve one at a time and then combine, rather than attempting the whole goal in a single step.

concepts.label: task parallelization
concepts.def_snippet: Task parallelization divides a job into independent subtasks that execute simultaneously across multiple processors or threads to reduce overall completion time.

concepts.label: Taylor Expansion
concepts.def_snippet: An approximation of a function near a point as a polynomial built from the function's derivatives there, with each added term capturing finer local detail such as slope, then curvature, then beyond.

concepts.label: Temperature
concepts.def_snippet: A scalar that rescales a model's output logits before they are turned into probabilities, controlling how random or deterministic the next-token choice is. Low temperature sharpens the distribution toward the most likely token; high temperature flattens it toward uniform.

concepts.label: Tensor
concepts.def_snippet: A multidimensional array of numbers that generalizes scalars, vectors, and matrices to any number of axes, and the central data structure of modern deep learning frameworks.

concepts.label: Test-Time Compute
concepts.def_snippet: The strategy of spending more computation at inference time, rather than only at training time, to improve a model's answers: generating longer reasoning, sampling many candidates, or searching, so accuracy scales with the compute spent per query.

concepts.label: The Bitter Lesson
concepts.def_snippet: Rich Sutton's 2019 observation that, over the history of AI, general methods that scale with computation have repeatedly beaten approaches that build in human knowledge by hand. The lesson is "bitter" because researchers keep relearning it.

concepts.label: Token Embedding
concepts.def_snippet: The lookup layer that maps each discrete token id to a dense, learned vector, turning a sequence of symbols into the continuous representations a transformer can actually compute over.

concepts.label: token optimization
concepts.def_snippet: Token optimization is a technique for reducing input or output token consumption in language model interactions through prompt refinement, caching, or selective information inclusi…

concepts.label: Tokenization
concepts.def_snippet: The step that breaks raw text into discrete units called tokens, the atomic symbols a language model actually reads and predicts, before they are mapped to vectors.

concepts.label: Tool Use
concepts.def_snippet: The general capability of a language model to extend itself by invoking external tools (search, code execution, APIs, databases) and folding their results back into its reasoning, rather than relying only on what it learned in training.

concepts.label: Top-k
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that keeps only the k most probable next tokens, discards the rest, renormalizes over the survivors, and samples from them. It caps how far into the tail the model is allowed to reach.

concepts.label: Top-p
concepts.def_snippet: A sampling method that restricts the next-token choice to the smallest set of tokens whose probabilities sum to a threshold p, then samples from that set. The candidate pool grows and shrinks with the model's confidence.

concepts.label: Transfer Learning
concepts.def_snippet: The principle of reusing knowledge a model learned on one task or dataset to improve learning on a different but related task, rather than training every new model from scratch.

concepts.label: Transformer
concepts.def_snippet: A neural network architecture built around self-attention rather than recurrence or convolution, which processes a whole sequence in parallel and has become the foundation of nearly every modern large language model.

concepts.label: Turing Test
concepts.def_snippet: A test of machine intelligence proposed by Alan Turing in 1950, in which a machine passes if a human judge cannot reliably distinguish its text conversation from a human's. It reframes "can machines think" as a question about observable behavior.

concepts.label: Underfitting
concepts.def_snippet: The failure mode in which a model is too simple to capture the underlying pattern in its data, so it performs poorly on both training and new data.

concepts.label: Universal Approximation Theorem
concepts.def_snippet: A family of results showing that a feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer of sufficient width can approximate any continuous function on a bounded domain to arbitrary accuracy. It establishes that neural networks are expressive in principle, while saying nothing about whether such a network can be learned.

concepts.label: Unsupervised Learning
concepts.def_snippet: A machine learning paradigm in which a model finds structure, patterns, or groupings in data that carries no labels, learning from the inputs alone.

concepts.label: Vanishing Gradient
concepts.def_snippet: A training pathology in deep networks where gradients shrink exponentially as they propagate backward through many layers, so early layers receive almost no learning signal and effectively stop training.

concepts.label: Variance
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of how far a random variable's values spread from its mean, defined as the expected squared deviation from the expectation, with the standard deviation as its square root.

concepts.label: Variational Autoencoder
concepts.def_snippet: A generative model that learns a probabilistic mapping between data and a continuous latent space, training an encoder and decoder jointly so that sampling from the latent prior and decoding produces new data.

concepts.label: VC Dimension
concepts.def_snippet: A measure of the capacity or expressive power of a class of models, defined as the size of the largest set of points that the class can label in all possible ways. It controls how many samples are needed to learn and how tightly training error predicts true error.

concepts.label: Vector
concepts.def_snippet: An ordered list of numbers that represents a point or a direction in space, and the fundamental object that machine learning uses to encode data, features, and model parameters.

concepts.label: Vector Database
concepts.def_snippet: A datastore built to index and query high-dimensional vectors by similarity, returning the stored vectors nearest a query vector quickly, usually via approximate nearest neighbor search plus metadata filtering.

concepts.label: Vector Space
concepts.def_snippet: A set of objects that can be added together and scaled by numbers while obeying a fixed list of consistency rules, providing the arena in which all of linear algebra and most machine learning representations live.

concepts.label: video generation
concepts.def_snippet: A video generation tool synthesizes realistic video sequences from text prompts, images, or other inputs by learning temporal and spatial patterns from training data.

concepts.label: vision-language models
concepts.def_snippet: Vision-language models are neural networks trained on paired image and text data to perform tasks requiring understanding of both visual content and natural language descriptions.

concepts.label: Weight Decay
concepts.def_snippet: A regularization technique that pulls the model's weights toward zero at every step, either by adding a penalty on their squared magnitude to the loss function or by shrinking them directly, which discourages overfitting and favors simpler solutions.

concepts.label: workflow orchestration
concepts.def_snippet: Workflow orchestration is a tool that automates the coordination and sequencing of multiple tasks or AI agents to complete complex processes end-to-end.

concepts.label: World Model
concepts.def_snippet: A learned internal model of how an environment evolves, which an agent uses to predict the consequences of actions and to plan or train inside its own imagination rather than only by acting in the real world.

concepts.label: world models
concepts.def_snippet: World models are learned representations of environmental dynamics that enable agents to predict future states and plan actions without real-world interaction.

concepts.label: YAML workflows
concepts.def_snippet: YAML workflows are configuration files that define multi-step processes for AI agents, specifying task sequences, conditions, and dependencies in a human-readable format.
